title: Investigate and patch 'throughput bleed' during regional surges impacting TTFT and tokens/sec across Hosted/Dedicated/Private
preview: Multiple customers across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments reported abrupt TTFT spikes and sustained tokens/sec degradation during regional traffic surges. Symptoms look like a 'throughput bleed' where initial-token latency climbs and steady-state token emission rate drops by 20-60% for affected variants. This ticket coordinates triage, a short-term patch, and follow-up hardening across runtime scheduler, KV cache policy, and kernel-selection heuristics.

title: Heterogeneous GPU headroom miscount causing silent dedicated shortfalls
preview: Multiple enterprise customers observed silent capacity shortfalls in Reserved Pools which manifested as increased cold-starts and higher p95 latency during burst traffic windows. Initial triage showed autoscaler did not add headroom quickly enough for tenants using GPU variants with lower vCPU equivalence, causing the scheduler to under-allocate across heterogeneous pools. This ticket tracks the investigation, immediate mitigations, hotfix rollout, and follow-up reliability work.

title: Alert onboarding checklist & triage journey mapper for Console
preview:
Goal: Design an onboarding checklist and a visual triage journey map inside the Redwood Console to reduce time-to-first-alert and improve on-call handovers. Motivation: new teams onboard to Redwood often miss key alert configuration steps (thresholds, notification routing, runbooks) which leads to noisy alerts, on-call confusion, and longer MTTR. This ticket covers a lightweight, stage-based checklist paired with an interactive triage timeline that surfaces runbooks, recent postmortem links, and a one-click ‘handoff snapshot’ for the next shift.

Scope: prototype UI patterns (checklist modal, stepper, contextual help, compact handoff snapshot), annotated copy for microcopy review, and a small usability test with 6 internal SRE/PM volunteers. Excludes backend integration work (alerts API wiring) — that will be a dependency owned by ENG.

Design principles: 1) Progressive disclosure: show essential steps first (create alert, set SLO target, routing) then advanced options (rate limits, enrichment). 2) Moment-based handoff: capture the state of an alert and top signals into a single snapshot tied to on-call shift. 3) Low-friction fallback: offer canned runbooks and templates to reduce cognitive load.

Deliverables: 1) High-fidelity Figma prototype for checklist + triage timeline; 2) Annotated handoff snapshot spec for engineers; 3) Usability test script + findings; 4) Accessibility notes and a short PM brief for rollout planning.

title: Admin Action Custody Flow: Data Residency Locking, Retention Policies, and Append-Only Exporter

This ticket covers: (1) a write-once exporter that pushes signed batches into region-scoped WORM buckets, (2) manifest generation with cryptographic custody stamps and retention markers, and (3) an automated evidence report that exports compliance-ready bundles for legal/forensic requests. Goal: ensure tamper-evident admin audit data with programmable retention and easy handoff for auditors.

title: Fix inconsistent final-token boundaries after SSE reconnects causing duplicate/missing tokens
preview:
Summary: Several customers reported truncated or duplicated tokens at the very end of Server-Sent Events (SSE) streams after a mid-stream disconnect + reconnect. This manifests as either a duplicated last token (client sees token N twice) or a missing token (client never receives token N). Affected: hosted API (primary), some Dedicated deployments behind our proxy layer, rare on Private when customers use older SDKs.

Observed during escalations: two enterprise customers (redacted) saw hallucination/regression in downstream parsers caused by duplicated tokens; a PLG customer reported lost final punctuation on short-generation use cases. These were reproducible in the lab by simulating a TCP reset during the final flush of the last chunk.

Root cause hypothesis: a race between the runtime's chunk flush (server side) and the resume-token/ack state machine. When an SSE connection closes mid-chunk, the server sometimes emits a final chunk and then marks the session resumable with a resume cursor based on the last acknowledged sequence. On reconnection, the client requests a resume from that cursor; however, if the server's emitted-but-not-acknowledged chunk overlapped the cursor boundary, the client can replay or miss tokens. Contributing factors: non-monotonic resume cursor generation in older kernel paths, and inconsistent guarantees between the HTTP proxy and runtime about when cursor persistence is durable.

Impact: breaches of deterministic output guarantees for streaming responses; downstream tooling that relies on exact token boundaries can mis-parse structured outputs. High severity for customers using structured outputs or exact-match evals.

Repro steps (lab):
1) Start a generation with SSE stream and set sequence logging (X-Stream-Debug: 1).
2) At approx 95% of the last chunk emitted, inject a TCP RST from client side.
3) Allow server to process the async flush and mark session resumable.
4) Reconnect with resume cursor; compare token stream received to original emitted stream.

Technical analysis so far:
- Traced server logs: tokens emitted with per-token sequence numbers but resume cursor persisted before final token offset was authoritative.
- Proxy layer sometimes buffered the final chunk and acknowledged resume persistence to control plane earlier than runtime durable write.
- Edge case triggered most frequently for short sequences with small chunk sizes (final token small).

Proposed fix (multiple coordinated changes):
1) Server: make resume cursor generation atomic with last-emitted-token-offset. Persist after flush callback; introduce a monotonic header X-RW-Stream-Seq that increments at token boundaries.
2) Proxy: stop acknowledging session resumability until runtime returns explicit durable commit. Add a short hysteresis window to avoid frequent toggles.
3) SDK: (backwards-compatible) attempt idempotent reassembly by using (stream-id, seq) dedupe on client; accept new X-RW-Stream-Seq header when present. Ship SDK patch that tolerates both old and new behavior.
4) Tests: add fuzz scenarios and chaos tests simulating mid-chunk TCP resets and delayed flushes. Add unit tests around cursor arithmetic for quantized/byte-based offsets.

Trade-offs considered:
- Delaying resume-ack at proxy adds small extra latency to failure handling (50-200ms) but avoids token duplication.
- Making resume cursor persistent synchronously increases durable writes; mitigated by batching commits for high-throughput flows and only enforcing sync for client-visible resumable session state.

Rollout plan: Canary rollout in hosted (us-west-2) at 1% traffic for 24h with detailed metrics; if stable, expand to 10% for 48h then global. Dedicated: staged advisory and patch (customers can opt-in) with backfill script. Private: document required SDK upgrade in release notes.

Metrics to monitor during rollout:
- Duplicate-token-rate (new metric): counts tokens with identical (stream-id, seq) seen by downstream clients per million tokens.
- Missing-final-token incidents (alerts from e2e checks).
- 90/95/99p tail latency for resume handshake.
- Error rate on streaming routes.

Rollback plan: revert X-RW-Stream-Seq enforcement and proxy acks; temporary mitigation will be to increase client-side dedupe window (SDK change) and enable conservative proxy delay toggle.

Documentation & artifacts: design doc, PR, customer escalations and ticket references included in links.

title: Weighted-donation failover for quota limiter with prefetch leases
preview:
Summary:
We need a robust mechanism to handle limiter outages and cross-region failovers that preserves SLOs and fairness during bursts. Current behavior: when a regional limiter is unhealthy or restarts, requests either queue up and spike synchronously at failover targets or are dropped inconsistently, causing customer-visible latency and unfair token consumption across tenants. Multi-region failovers sometimes cause duplicate quota allocation or starvation depending on which limiter hears the request first.

Proposal (high level):
1) Prefetch leases: limiters can issue short-duration 'leases' to upstream routing and edge proxies that represent a guaranteed allowance of N tokens/requests for a specific key (org/token). Leases are cheap bearer tokens that are valid for a small TTL (e.g., 2s) and do not require synchronous coordination for every request.
2) Weighted donation on recovery: when a limiter is returning from degraded/outage mode, it calculates unsatisfied demand during outage from sampled admission logs and performs a weighted-donation handoff to neighboring healthy regions. This donation is expressed as ephemeral lease grants to avoid double-counting on the source limiter.
3) Circuit-breaker integration: add an SLO-aware circuit-breaker that degrades enforcement into a conservative soft-limit mode during cross-region failover windows. Soft-limit mode enforces fairness heuristics (weighted-per-customer share) instead of strict token denial to keep 95% tail latency within SLOs while shedding proportionally.
4) Backpressure API signals: routers should expose Retry-After and X-Rate-Limit-* headers with lease metadata so SDKs and clients can apply adaptive retry/backoff.

Design details:
- Lease format: signed JWT-lite including (key, lease_id, tokens, expiry_ms, region, version). Keep size small to pass through CDN headers.
- Allocation algorithm: when issuing leases, limiters prefer to allocate against current token buckets but may prefetch up to 'prefetch_limit' (configurable per tier). Prefetch protects against short blips and bursty clients.
- Weighted donation: donor region computes shortage per tenant weighted by historical effective throughput and error budget. Donations limited to donor's recovered capacity and configured donor_fraction to avoid starving local traffic during global events.
- Consistency guard: receivers validate lease signatures + idempotency table for recent lease_ids (TTL-bound) to avoid double-apply. Receiver maintains small LRU cache of recent lease_ids to dedupe.
- Rollout flags: feature gated by environment and customer tier. Default off for small accounts, opt-in for early testing.

Testing and validation plan:
- Unit tests for lease issuance, signature validation, and dedupe behavior.
- Integration tests with edge proxy simulator to ensure headers and tokens are honored.
- Chaos scenario: kill regional limiter X and measure tail latency and token fairness when failover donation occurs.
- Load tests: run synthetic multi-tenant burst profile and assert 99th latency and fairness metric within SLOs.

Monitoring and SLO mapping:
- New metrics: leases_issued_total, leases_consumed_total, lease_dedupe_rejects, donation_rate_per_region, donation_applied_success, soft_limit_shed_rate.
- Dashboards: per-region lease utilization, donor/receiver counts, fairness_ratio (90th/50th throughput per tenant during events).
- Alerts: if leases_dedupe_rejects > 0.5% over 1m or donation_applied_success < 95% during rollout, page oncall.
- SLO impact: aim to keep 99th latency increase < 10% and error budget burn no more than 2x during controlled failover windows.

Rollout plan:
1) Design review and proto (this ticket) -> 1 week.
2) Backend prototype in limiter service behind feature flag + unit tests -> 2 weeks.
3) Edge proxy header passthrough and SDK changes (show lease headers, expose Retry-After hints) -> parallel 1 week.
4) Canary in non-prod then low-traffic prod region with a handful of engineering-run tenants -> 1 week.
5) Expand to multi-region controlled failovers and run chaos -> 2 weeks.
6) Full release for dedicated + hosted with opt-in customers -> gated by metrics.

Risks and mitigations:
- Double consumption: mitigate via lease idempotency and short TTLs. Perform a small-scale audit comparing billing vs applied tokens after rollouts.
- Complexity in donation algorithm leading to unfairness: expose donation debug mode that logs per-tenant weights and a simulation endpoint for PM and SRE to validate before enabling.
- Security: signed leases and rotating signing keys; require security review before enabling for enterprise customers.

Dependencies:
- Edge proxy changes to propagate and accept lease headers.
- SDK updates to surface Retry-After/lease semantics to app developers.
- Key management for signing leases (KMS rotation).

Acceptance criteria:
- Durable lease issuance with <1% reject under high load in canary.
- Fairness ratio improved vs current baseline by at least 30% during synthetic cross-region outage tests.
- No customer-visible billing discrepancies in audit sample post-rollout.

Notes: prototype design doc: https://confluence.redwood-inference.internal/rate-limiter/prefetch-leases-donation (internal). GitHub proto PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/limiter/pull/4523. Grafana draft dash: https://grafana.redwood-inference.internal/d/limiter/lease-ops

title: Standardize retry/timeout/backoff behavior and idempotency across services & SDKs
preview: Problem statement: disparate retry/timeout/backoff implementations across microservices and language SDKs are causing unpredictable tail latency and duplicate side-effects during multi-region failovers. We need a cross-team standard that: 1) defines default retry counts, backoff strategies, and timeouts tuned for our SLO targets; 2) mandates idempotency key semantics for mutating endpoints; 3) provides opt-in hedged request support for latency-sensitive routes; and 4) integrates with circuit breakers and load-shedding policies for overload protection. Scope: core HTTP/gRPC services, Redwood SDKs (JS, Python, Go), and gateway/proxy layer. Out of scope: third-party integrations and legacy internal admin tooling (handled in follow-ups).

title: Investigate HPA/KEDA sensitivity drift causing under-scaling during warm-start bursts
preview:
Summary: On 2026-03-10 we observed repeated under-scaling of model serving pools during multiple short warm-start bursts for the text-generation fleet in us-west-2. Symptoms were elevated 95th and 99th percentile latency spikes while overall CPU/GPU utilization remained well below autoscale thresholds. The effect manifested as degraded tail latency for a subset of customers (~2% of production traffic) for ~25 minutes.

What happened (brief): A change in KEDA scaling behavior combined with a drift in the HPA metric window exposed a gap where rapid bursts of new sessions (warm-starts that populate KV cache) caused transient token-generation latency to spike, but the scaling system did not react quickly enough due to the scaler's debounce/windowing configuration and a higher-than-expected metric noise floor. Our existing safeguards (minReplicas, fallback routing) did not fully prevent latency impact because the bursts were localized to a new routing shard.

Root cause: Three contributing factors: 1) KEDA Prometheus scaler configuration was tightened in runtime-1.18 to reduce false positives, which increased the effective debounce and caused under-responsive scale-ups for short bursts. 2) The HPA’s metrics collection window (60s) plus the scaler thresholding produced a quantization effect where short spikes were averaged out. 3) Prefix/KV cache warmup creates a short-lived high-rt phase per-request that inflates per-pod latency but doesn't immediately raise aggregate CPU/GPU metrics used by HPA. The combined effect is a false-negative scaling decision for bursty warm-start patterns.

Detection: Engineers on-call alerted via latency SLO slack monitor at 2026-03-10T14:02Z. Automated runbooks flagged elevated tail latencies and created incident INC-2026-0310-09. The console showed a single shard experiencing repeated 95p token latency > 1.2s while throughput matched expected request rates.

Impact: Medium customer impact for ~25 minutes affecting routing shard s-west-11: ~2% of live requests experienced higher token tail latency causing several interactive flows to time out and some retries. No PII/data loss.

Immediate mitigation taken: 1) Increased minReplicas for the shard from 2 -> 4 to provide headroom while we investigated. 2) Created an emergency canary routing rule to divert new sessions away from the affected shard. 3) Reverted the most recent Prometheus scaler debounce change (rollback to previous KEDA config) as a stop-gap.

Post-incident findings: The hardening change to KEDA reduced false scaling spikes for steady traffic but made the system brittle for short-lived warm-start bursts. Our HPA metrics and KEDA scaler were tuned to optimize for sustained load; they did not account for high-frequency, low-duration bursts that are common in interactive model usage (e.g., demo/test flows, short multi-user bursts). Observability gaps: we lacked a simple derived metric for "warm-start burst index" (rate of new sessions with KV cache misses) and we don't currently expose a per-routing-shard scaling sensitivity dashboard.

Recommendations (high level): Introduce a burst-aware scaling profile, add a short-timescale scaler (fast-path) for warm-start signals, implement probe smoothing for HPA metric noise, expand per-shard observability, and codify emergency routing fallbacks to trigger automatically when shard-tail-latency crosses thresholds.

Links: PR with emergency rollback: https://github.com/redwood-inference/runtime/pull/1289, Incident docket: https://internal.redwood.io/incidents/INC-2026-0310-09, Dashboard snapshot: https://console.redwood.io/dashboards/latency-snapshots/2026-03-10-14

Our HPA metrics and KEDA scaler were tuned to optimize for sustained load; they did not account for high-frequency, low-duration bursts that are common in interactive model usage (e.g., demo/test flows, short multi-user bursts). Observability gaps: we lacked a simple derived metric for "warm-start burst index" (rate of new sessions with KV cache misses) and we don't currently expose a per-routing-shard scaling sensitivity dashboard.

Recommendations (high level): Introduce a burst-aware scaling profile, add a short-timescale scaler (fast-path) for warm-start signals, implement probe smoothing for HPA metric noise, expand per-shard observability, and codify emergency routing fallbacks to trigger automatically when shard-tail-latency crosses thresholds.

title: Revoked API key reintroduced after deployment rollback, causing temporary privilege resurfacing
preview: Customer-reported incidents across Dedicated and Private deployments: several customers observed that API keys previously revoked were briefly accepted again following a control-plane deployment rollback. Affected surfaces: API key auth in gateway, session reconciliation on dedicated pools, and private control planes with local cache replicas.

title: GPU slice gateway and tenant quota sentinel
preview:
Problem: Dedicated customers are experiencing unpredictable tail latency and throughput degradation when noisy tenants temporarily consume disproportionate GPU bandwidth. Current coarse hard-quotas and tenant lane reservation designs either underutilize fleet capacity or cause abrupt preemptions that break customer SLAs.

Goal: Implement a lightweight admission-and-control plane ("GPU Slice Gateway") plus a tenant quota sentinel service that enforces temporal throughput guarantees, provides graceful degradation, and drives autoscaling triggers with predictive noisy-neighbor suppression. The solution should prioritize tenant separation, minimize noisy neighbor impact, and allow transparent quota accounting for reporting and billing.

Scope: Implement control-plane components (gateway + sentinel), runtime enforcement hooks (cgroup/ML runtime pacing integration), telemetry & signals for noisy-neighbor detection, autoscale policy integration for Dedicated pools, and an operator playbook for escalations and rollbacks. This ticket covers the core gateway + sentinel MVP and integration tests; follow-ups will handle UI/console visibility and billing pipeline integration.

title: Quantile-driven stream adaptive scheduler for heterogeneous turn lengths
preview:
Background: Production traffic exhibits heavy heterogeneity in prompt vs response lengths: many short prompt-only calls interleaved with long-generation responses. Our current continuous-batching admission and KV-reservation heuristics optimize for average token density and suffer in two ways: 1) p99 latency spikes when long turns monopolize slots and fragment GPU slab memory, and 2) sustained KV-residency increases memory fragmentation, reducing effective batch capacity and increasing per-token cost.

Goal: Implement a quantile-driven stream admission controller and per-cohort KV-reservation adaptor that dynamically adjusts admission thresholds and KV retention targets to reduce p99 latency and lower token-costs for mixed prompt/response workloads without degrading p50 throughput. This ticket covers design, prototype, evaluation on synthetic and shadow traffic, and an incremental rollout plan with telemetry and guardrails.

High-level approach:
- Quantile-driven admission: compute online quantiles (p50,p90,p95,p99) over token-lengths and response lengths per customer-cohort. Admission thresholds shift according to quantile drift rather than mean.
- Cohort assignment: lightweight hashing by API key + route to assign requests into cohorts for targeted KV reservations and admission policies.
- Adaptive KV-reservation: allocate KV cache "soft reservations" as a fraction of observed cohort working set; reservations decay with usage half-life and can be preempted under global memory pressure.
- GPU slab-aware packing hints: expose per-slot hints to serving runtime to prefer co-locating similar-length turns into slabs, reducing fragmentation and improving kernel packing efficiency.
- Tail hedge preemption: short requests can preempt long-stream slots when latency SLOs are threatened; the scheduler supports non-blocking preemption with resume tokens for long streams to continue later.
- Continuous calibration loop: compare predicted vs observed tail latencies and adjust admission quantiles and reservation factors every 5 minutes with dampening to avoid oscillations.

Design decisions and trade-offs considered: 
- Per-cohort granularity vs configuration explosion: we use coarse cohorts (by API key and route) to balance policy fidelity and storage/compute costs for streaming stats.
- Soft reservations only: favors availability over strict isolation to avoid wasted capacity during transient traffic drops.
- Preemption complexity: support resumable long streams by checkpointing KV cursors; this adds overhead but improves tail latency for interactive workloads.

Implementation plan (milestones):
1) Instrumentation: add streaming-length histograms and quantile estimator (CKMS) into front-line gateways and batcher metrics. (2w)
2) Admission controller prototype: server-side component that consumes quantiles and emits per-slot admission flags. Unit tests + local simulator. (2w)
3) KV-adaptor: implement soft reservation bookkeeping and eviction priority adjustments in runtime. Integrate with existing KV GC. (3w)
4) Scheduler hints & slab packing: implement hint channel and runtime adopter; add microbenchmarks for packing efficiency. (2w)
5) Canary & shadow tests: run shadow traffic with production traces, compare p50/p95/p99, kv-hit-rate, GPU utilization, and cost estimates. (2w)
6) Incremental rollout: 10% -> 40% -> 100% per region with automated rollback on SLO regressions. (2w)

Metrics to track:
- Latency: p50/p90/p95/p99 generation latency per cohort and global
- Throughput: tokens/sec, requests/sec, batch formation latency
- Cost: token-cost estimated, GPU utilization, slot occupancy efficiency
- KV metrics: kv-hit-rate, kv-residency-per-cohort, eviction rate, fragmentation events (slab holes)
- Failure modes: preemption-related resume failures, excessive oscillation in admission thresholds

Acceptance criteria:
- p99 gen latency reduced by >= 25% on mixed-turn synthetic trace vs baseline while p50 change within ±5%
- Token-cost per request reduced by >= 8% on representative workload in shadow testing
- KV-residency variance across cohorts reduced by >= 20% without increasing eviction rate > 10% relative to baseline
- No resume-failure incidents during a 72-hour 10% canary run and rollbacks automated on >10% SLO breach

Rollout plan and safety:
- Start with non-customer-facing shadow runs and internal dashboards for 7 days.
- 10% canary for low-risk customers (no strict SLA) with conservative reservation factors. Monitor for 72 hours.
- Gradually expand with automated rollback triggers (p99 increase > 10% or kv-eviction spike > 20%).

Risks and mitigations: 
- Oscillation: use exponential moving average with hysteresis for control signals. Add circuit-breaker to freeze policy if oscillation detected.
- Resume complexity bugs: add fuzz tests and record/replay harness to exercise preemption/resume paths.
- Memory regression: enforce global slab watermarks and emergency compaction policy.

Links and artifacts: 
- Design doc (Confluence): https://redwood.atlassian.net/l/c/QUANTILE-SCHEDULER-DOC (internal)
- Simulator and benchmarks (GitHub): https://github.com/redwood-inference/continuous-batcher/tree/quantile-admit-proto
- Dashboard (Grafana): https://grafana.redwood.company/d/continuous-batcher/quantile-admit

Notes: Prototype focuses on minimizing runtime dependencies; heavy logic lives in control plane to keep runtime path fast.

title: Telemetry-driven Canary Policy Engine with Safety Slicing and Auto-Remediation
preview:
Summary:
Build a policy engine for canary rollouts that uses fine-grained safety 'slices' (cohorts defined by traffic attributes) combined with telemetry-driven feedback to adapt step schedules, traffic splits, and automatic remediation (rollback or mitigation). This ticket scopes the runtime config language, evaluator, guardrail triggers, and integration points with Console telemetry and rollout API.

Goals:
- Provide a compact declarative rollout spec that supports: staged traffic splits, cohort-based overrides, shadow probing, and adaptive step extension or rollback based on live telemetry.
- Implement a safety-slicing system to define cohorts (by tenant, geolocation, request-size, prompt-hash buckets, model-variant) and enforce per-slice SLO/thresholds.
- Build a feedback loop: live telemetry drives step duration, step size, and triggers (pause/rollback/mitigation).
- Surface decisions and tradeoffs in Console (per-slice dashboards and decision trace).

User stories:
- As an SRE, I can define a canary that ramps 0% -> 1% -> 5% -> 25% with per-step duration automatically extended if slice error rates increase but remain borderline.
- As a PM, I can mark certain customer cohorts as high-priority safety slices that should never exceed 5% error increase without automated rollback.
- As an engineer, I can run offline simulations of a rollout spec against historical telemetry to predict failure modes.

Key design decisions (so far):
- Safety slices are first-class in the rollout spec. A slice selector supports attributes: customer_id, region, route, prompt_length_bucket, token_count_bucket, and hashed cohort buckets.
- Telemetry inputs: latency_p50/p95, error_rate (4xx/5xx/model-failure), hallucination_score (from eval hooks), semantic-drift (embedding distance vs golden set), and cost_per_token.
- Rollout evaluator runs in the control plane; decisions are authoritative but short-lived actions executed at the edge (routing proxies).
- Auto-remediation actions: PAUSE, ROLL BACK (immediate), THROTTLE (slice-level cap), SWITCH-TO-FALLBACK (lower-capacity model variant).
- Default: conservative behavior for P1 canaries - automatically PAUSE on medium violations and ROLLBACK on high violations.

Rollout spec example (declarative):

rollout: {
  name: "canary/evg-adapter-2026-03",
  steps: [
    { percent: 1, min_time: "15m" },
    { percent: 5, min_time: "30m" },
    { percent: 25, min_time: "1h" }
  ],
  safety_slices: [
    { id: "enterprise-tenants", selector: "tenant in [\"acme\", \"globex\"]", fail_action: "rollback", sla: { error_rate_delta: 0.01 } },
    { id: "short-prompts", selector: "prompt_length < 64", fail_action: "pause", sla: { latency_p95_delta: 0.20 } }
  ],
  telemetry_policy: {
    lookback: "5m",
    evaluation_interval: "1m",
    thresholds: {
      medium: { error_rate_delta: 0.005, latency_p95_delta: 0.15 },
      high: { error_rate_delta: 0.02, latency_p95_delta: 0.30 }
    },
    action_rules: [
      { level: "medium", action: "extend_step", extend_by: "2x", reason: "marginal" },
      { level: "high", action: "rollback", reason: "safety_violation" }
    ]
  },
  shadow_probes: {
    enabled: true,
    sample_rate: 0.05,
    probe_checks: ["prefix_similarity", "regression_eval_score"]
  }
}

Safety enforcement semantics:
- Per-step evaluation: For each safety slice, compute delta vs baseline for configured metrics. If any slice exceeds 'high' thresholds in the lookback window, trigger rollback. If exceeding 'medium' thresholds, extend the min_time and mark step as under review; optionally call a mitigation (throttle/switch fallback).
- Slice precedence: explicit slice fail_action overrides global action. Highest-priority slice that matches wins.
- Shadow probe fast-fail: If shadow probes detect a regression above configured severity for a slice, escalate to 'high' immediately regardless of overall percent.

Telemetry -> Decision flow:
1) Metrics emitted from runtime (token-level latency, error counters, eval hooks) flow into Console metrics + an aggregated rollout-evaluator stream.
2) Evaluator computes per-slice stats and compares to baseline (rolling baseline from last 7d or explicit baseline snapshot).
3) Decisions (extend/pause/rollback/mitigate) are recorded with causal explainability metadata and emitted to the control-plane audit log and Console.

Observability and runbook hooks:
- Console endpoints: per-rollout timeline, per-slice SLO sparkline, decision trace (why a step was extended or rolled back), and a knob to manually override/eject a slice.
- Alerts: Pager duty on 'rollback' for high-customer-impact slices; slack channel + ephemeral badge for medium events.
- Runbook: link to playbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Canary+Runbook

Simulation & QA results (initial run):
- Ran 30 synthetic rollout simulations against 14-day historical traffic for two model variants. Simulated 'spike-latency' and 'semantic-drift' faults.
- Results: safety-slicing correctly prevented exposure of the 'enterprise-tenants' slice in 98% of trials and triggered rollback in 92% of trials when drift > 0.25 embedding-distance.
- Identified noise for very small slices (<0.2% traffic) where p-value is unstable; added a minimum-sample-size guard to avoid false positives.

Open tradeoffs and discussion items:
- Baseline calculation: rolling-window baseline is responsive but can adapt away from regressions; snapshot baseline is stable but requires manual refresh. Proposed hybrid: rolling with decay + snapshot anchor when a canary is created.
- False positive mitigation for low-traffic slices: we plan to use hierarchical aggregation (slice -> super-slice -> global) to avoid spurious rollbacks.
- Where to run the evaluator: central control-plane allows single-source-of-truth for policy but adds latency to decision loop; edge evaluators could act faster but increase operational complexity. For MVP use central evaluator.

Implementation notes:
- Backend: extend rollout-service with a new evaluator module (golang) that consumes telemetry from the metric pipeline (Kafka).
- Runtime: add an API for routing proxies to request per-route traffic allocations for the current rollout and slice caps.
- DB: store rollout specs and decision traces in Postgres (append-only timeline table) for auditability.
- RBAC: only users with 'rollout.admin' may create specs that auto-rollback enterprise slices.

PRs & artifacts:
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Telemetry+Canary+Policy+Engine
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rollout-service/pull/415 (evaluator skeleton)
- Simulation harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rollout-sim

Recent progress / updates:
- 2026-03-05: Initial design review completed; PM and SRE aligned on slice semantics. Action: add minimum-sample-size and baseline anchoring. (Reviewer: Lena Fischer)
- 2026-03-09: Prototype evaluator ingests synthetic telemetry and emits decision traces to Console. Manual rollback action implemented. (PR: #415)
- 2026-03-12: QA found noisy rollbacks for sub-0.2% slices; implemented min-sample-size and hierarchical aggregation. Added default 'cooldown' on rollback actions to prevent flip-flop.

Next steps / Acceptance criteria:
- Implement per-slice throttle and fallback switch actions and wire to routing proxies (ETA 2026-03-28).
- End-to-end test against staging traffic with a canary spec that includes 3 safety slices and shadow probing (pass criteria: no customer-visible latency regressions after 10000 simulated requests; correct rollback on injected faults).
- Security review completed and documented (required=true).

Follow-ups / future work (out of scope for this ticket):
- Distributed/edge evaluator prototype for sub-second decisions.
- ML-based anomaly scoring to replace fixed thresholds.
- UI: visual authoring of safety slices and guided suggestions for thresholds based on historical variance.

Owners & reviewers:
- Owner: Ethan Rivera (engineering)
- PM: Maya Chen
- SRE reviewer: Omar Haddad
- Design reviewer: Lena Fischer

Notes: keep config grammar compact and extensible; prioritize deterministic safety outcomes over aggressive rollouts for the first two sprints.

3) Decisions (extend/pause/rollback/mitigate) are recorded with causal explainability metadata and emitted to the control-plane audit log and Console.

Observability and runbook hooks:
- Console endpoints: per-rollout timeline, per-slice SLO sparkline, decision trace (why a step was extended or rolled back), and a knob to manually override/eject a slice.
- Alerts: Pager duty on 'rollback' for high-customer-impact slices; slack channel + ephemeral badge for medium events.
- Runbook: link to playbook: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Canary+Runbook

Simulation & QA results (initial run):
- Ran 30 synthetic rollout simulations against 14-day historical traffic for two model variants. Simulated 'spike-latency' and 'semantic-drift' faults.
- Results: safety-slicing correctly prevented exposure of the 'enterprise-tenants' slice in 98% of trials and triggered rollback in 92% of trials when drift > 0.25 embedding-distance.
- Identified noise for very small slices (<0.2% traffic) where p-value is unstable; added a minimum-sample-size guard to avoid false positives.

Open tradeoffs and discussion items:
- Baseline calculation: rolling-window baseline is responsive but can adapt away from regressions; snapshot baseline is stable but requires manual refresh. Proposed hybrid: rolling with decay + snapshot anchor when a canary is created.
- False positive mitigation for low-traffic slices: we plan to use hierarchical aggregation (slice -> super-slice -> global) to avoid spurious rollbacks.
- Where to run the evaluator: central control-plane allows single-source-of-truth for policy but adds latency to decision loop; edge evaluators could act faster but increase operational complexity. For MVP use central evaluator.

Implementation notes:
- Backend: extend rollout-service with a new evaluator module (golang) that consumes telemetry from the metric pipeline (Kafka).
- Runtime: add an API for routing proxies to request per-route traffic allocations for the current rollout and slice caps.
- DB: store rollout specs and decision traces in Postgres (append-only timeline table) for auditability.
- RBAC: only users with 'rollout.admin' may create specs that auto-rollback enterprise slices.

PRs & artifacts:
- Design doc: https://confluence.redwood.ai/display/INF/Telemetry+Canary+Policy+Engine
- Prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rollout-service/pull/415 (evaluator skeleton)
- Simulation harness: https://github.com/redwood-inference/rollout-sim

Recent progress / updates:
- 2026-03-05: Initial design review completed; PM and SRE aligned on slice semantics. Action: add minimum-sample-size and baseline anchoring. (Reviewer: Lena Fischer)
- 2026-03-09: Prototype evaluator ingests synthetic telemetry and emits decision traces to Console. Manual rollback action implemented. (PR: #415)
- 2026-03-12: QA found noisy rollbacks for sub-0.2% slices; implemented min-sample-size and hierarchical aggregation. Added default 'cooldown' on rollback actions to prevent flip-flop.

Next steps / Acceptance criteria:
- Implement per-slice throttle and fallback switch actions and wire to routing proxies (ETA 2026-03-28).
- End-to-end test against staging traffic with a canary spec that includes 3 safety slices and shadow probing (pass criteria: no customer-visible latency regressions after 10000 simulated requests; correct rollback on injected faults).
- Security review completed and documented (required=true).

Follow-ups / future work (out of scope for this ticket):
- Distributed/edge evaluator prototype for sub-second decisions.
- ML-based anomaly scoring to replace fixed thresholds.
- UI: visual authoring of safety slices and guided suggestions for thresholds based on historical variance.

title: Private packaging experiment: split license + ops-credit model, tiered support SLAs, and scheduled upgrade windows with SQ streamlining
preview:
Background: Enterprise prospects evaluating Private/VPC or on-prem deployments frequently request predictable TCO, clear upgrade cadence, and a scoped security questionnaire (SQ) turnaround. Current packaging conversations stall on three fronts: (1) license fee framing (flat annual vs usage-adjusted commit), (2) operational support entitlements and response SLAs, and (3) upgrade/patch cadence commitments for air-gapped customers.

Objective: Run a six-week experiment to validate a modular packaging construct that (a) splits the offering into Base License + Ops Credits, (b) defines three support tiers with concrete SLA/RPO/RTO and add-on expedited patching, and (c) commits to a scheduled upgrade window approach (quarterly minor + semiannual major) with an emergency patch path. Concurrently, streamline enterprise SQ responses by producing an SQ playbook and a checklist that reduces legal/security review turnaround by 40% for standard packages.

Success metrics:
- Win rate lift: +12% conversion on enterprise Private deals where pricing presented as split license + ops credits within 90 days.
- Time to close: reduce negotiation cycles by 20% for deals > $250k ARR.
- SQ turnaround: average SQ response time reduced from ~18 business days to <=11 business days for standard package.
- Support opex predictability: customers adopting Ops Credits model show <=15% variance vs committed spend over first 12 months.

Scope of experiment:
- Pricing models to validate: (A) Annual Base License (per-node band) + Ops Credits (monthly burnable credits for runtime/ops/patches); (B) Usage-indexed license (token/throughput) with optional Ops Credits; (C) Flat all-in subscription with lower priority SLAs for SMB enterprise.
- Support tiers: Bronze (business-hours email, 48h response), Silver (24/7 critical, 4h P1 engage), Gold (24/7 critical+accelerated patching SLA, 1h P1 engage, quarterly on-site/remote review).
- Upgrade cadence proposal: Quarterly minor updates (opt-in automated), Semiannual major upgrades scheduled in protected windows with a documented rollback plan, Emergency patch SLA (Gold customers eligible for out-of-cycle patches with ops credit surcharge).
- SQ playbook: curated artifact answering top 25 security questions in template form, mapping of required evidence (logs, config screenshots, architecture diagram), and a prioritized escalation path for non-standard asks.

Stakeholders: Sales AE/AM (trial customers), Legal (contract language), Security & Compliance (SQ validation), Engineering (packaging feasibility and automated upgrade tooling), Support (runbooks & escalation), Finance (forecasting revenue recognition & ops credit accounting).

Risks & mitigations:
- Risk: Customers misinterpret Ops Credits as variable cost spike. Mitigation: provide burn forecasts, credit top-ups with caps, and 30-day usage dashboards.
- Risk: Engineering velocity to support scheduled windows. Mitigation: commit only to windows backed by an internal release guardrail and gated rollouts for private instances.
- Risk: SQ answers leak sensitive implementation specifics. Mitigation: redacted evidence templates and NDAs where necessary.

Next steps (first 2 weeks):
1) Finalize 3-pack pricing bands and Ops Credit denominations with Finance.
2) Draft contract addendum language for modular license + ops credits and expedited patching clause with Legal.
3) Produce first-cut SQ playbook (top 25 items) with Security & Solutions Engineering.
4) Pilot with 3 active prospects (AE assignments) and instrument win/loss reasons in CRM.

title: SSO group-merge race causing mixed allow/deny and intermittent permission-denied for admin users
preview:
Summary: Several enterprise customers reported intermittent 'permission denied' errors for accounts that should have retained admin privileges after SSO group synchronizations. Issue occurs when IdP group membership changes and our SSO connector performs a concurrent group merge/update while RBAC v2 is reconciling grants. The race produces a transient state where allow rules from one source are applied but deny rules from another are not fully reconciled, leaving an effective deny for certain admin actions.

Reproduction notes:
- Steps to reproduce observed in prod test: 1) User A is in IdP Group 'Org-Admins' and a local RBAC role maps that group to 'org_admin' with full permissions. 2) During a bulk group update from the IdP (group rename/merge across OUs), the SSO connector issues overlapping add/remove events. 3) The RBAC v2 comparator merges deltas out-of-order: remove(GroupX)->apply; then add(GroupY)->apply while audit/permission cache has stale deny mask. 4) User A attempts admin-only endpoint (console rollout toggle) and receives 403 despite UI showing 'org_admin'.

Root cause hypothesis: Race between the SSO event processor and RBAC permission evaluation path when audit-cache TTL is short and permission writes are not fully atomic across two code paths (group-mapping updater vs policy ingestion worker). Two contributing bugs: (1) the group-merge code path uses eventual consistency writes to PermissionsStore without ensuring versioned compare-and-swap, (2) audit log replay path applies policy deltas out-of-order when merging SAML assertion-derived deny rules.

Impact: Affected customers see intermittent admin 403s during IdP mass updates or large org migrations. This primarily affects onboarding/offboarding windows and can block canary rollouts and emergency admin interventions. Multiple customers escalated (support tickets #C-9112, #C-9120) and requested hotfix.

Acceptance criteria / success metrics:
- No 403s for users with effective admin grants during IdP group sync windows in the staging canaries for 72 hours.
- Audit logs show consistent, versioned permission delta ordering and an explicit CAS token per policy write.
- E2E tests covering concurrent group-add/remove cycles pass in CI with stress mode enabled.

Planned fix:
1) Add optimistic concurrency control to PermissionsStore writes (versioned CAS token) so group-mapping updates are atomic.
2) Change SSO event processor to batch group delta events for the same subject for 250ms to reduce interleaving.
3) Ensure audit-log replay order is strictly serial per-subject using sequence IDs from SAML assertions and IdP event stream.
4) Add a short-lived 'grace read-through' on permission evaluation that consults the pending-update marker before returning a 403 for admin-scoped routes.

Longer-term: Revisit RBAC v2 consistency model: consider a single canonical ingestion path for external identity events and deprecate parallel writers. We should also extend the audit log schema with a 'causal_id' to trace policy writes back to IdP events.

Notes: This ticket intersects with ENG-10284 (audit role mismatch edge cases) and ENG-13021 (invite/role shadowing) — those contain related investigation notes and test harnesses.

title: Auto-restore coordinator for risky config flips and batching/caching policy changes
preview:
Goal: Build an automated 'restore coordinator' that detects harmful config flips (batching parameters, KV/prefix cache toggles, quantization profiles, autoscale knobs) and performs safe, auditable restore actions with minimal user intervention. The system will combine econometric signals (per-token cost delta), SLO degradation detectors (p50/p95 latency, error-rate), and semantic regressions from lightweight evals to decide whether to restore previous config state. Key requirements: 1) low false positive restores, 2) explainable decision logs, 3) safe execution with canary/shadow simulation prior to any live restore, 4) reconciliation so long-running requests and KV cache state are consistent after a restore.

Scope: initial MVP targets hosted and Dedicated controls for batching/window-size and KV-cache enable/disable; later phases will add quantization profile flips, autoscaler policy swaps, and private deployment orchestration. The coordinator will integrate with Console rollouts (to observe route-level metrics), the runtime health API (for in-flight state), and the change application pipeline (to execute restores or staged replays).

Design considerations and trade-offs: avoid aggressive immediate rollbacks that could oscillate when underlying noise causes transient metric blips. Use hysteresis and economic thresholds: only trigger when cost rises > 8% AND latency SLOs breached for > 5 minutes across at least two regions or routes. To reduce blast radius, prefer staged restores (canary back to previous config for 5% of traffic for 10 minutes), with automatic escalation to full restore if canary shows improvement. Use shadow-apply simulator to run config in parallel without affecting production to validate KV cache warming and prefix-hit rates.

State reconciliation: when restoring, reconcile KV/prefix caches by: 1) marking affected nodes as 'quarantine' to avoid stale cache hits, 2) replaying a bounded list of recent requests into warmed caches in read-only mode, and 3) triggering a gentle TTL-based invalidation where necessary. We will preserve in-flight generator session tokens by allowing dual execution paths for up to 30s while draining connections.

Observability and audit: every decision must emit a structured decision record (policy-id, metric-signals, simulation outcome, canary results) and link to Console incident page and the implicated rollout. Provide tooling in Console to replay the simulation and to approve/override automated restores.

Safety / governance: expose a user-configurable opt-out per-customer; enterprise customers can opt into manual approval only. All automated restores require an audit entry and optional Jira/Linear comment creation.

Deliverables: design doc on Confluence, runtime API contract, coordinator service with integration tests and canary rollout, Console UI for overrides and logs, playbook and SLA notes.

Links: Confluence design draft: https://redwood.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/ENG/pages/5234012/auto-restore-coordinator, prototype PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/platform/pull/4382, telemetry dashboard: https://app.datadog.com/dash/redwood/runtime/auto-restore, incident learning: INC-2026-03-batchcache-regression.

title: Orbit Release Sentinel: Safe versioned rollouts and traffic modulation for Hosted API
preview:
Problem: Our Hosted API deployment pipeline currently lacks a unified mechanism to (1) publish versioned model/runtime artifacts to LTS/edge channels, (2) enforce automated rollout gates that consider regional SLOs and cost tiers, and (3) perform incremental traffic modulation between blue/green pairs with safe automated fallback. This has led to manual cutovers, ad-hoc regional throttles, and one incident where a skewed rollout caused increased 95p latency for a key EU customer.

Objective: Implement an "Orbit Release Sentinel" — a release-engineering component and associated runbook that manages version metadata, channel tagging (canary / stable / lts), per-region rollout gates, and traffic-modulation primitives (attenuate, reflect, abort) integrated with our runtime, routing layer, and console. The sentinel must support automated policy evaluation, staged traffic shifts, SLO-driven aborts, and clear observability for on-call and product owners.

title: Multischema mux: decoder regression and streaming checkpoint validation suite
preview: Create an automated regression and smoke test suite that validates JSON mode enforcement and constrained-decoder behavior when a single request may negotiate different schema versions or switch schema mid-stream due to tool/function calls. The suite must cover: (1) streaming partial JSON that must be reassembled and validated at checkpoints, (2) tool-return payloads that introduce a different schema version and trigger dynamic schema selection, (3) constrained decoding enforcement under token-level penalties and sampling noise, and (4) compatibility fallbacks where the runtime substitutes a compatible schema adapter. Tests should run in CI against the latest decoder and the compatibility shim. Acceptance criteria: deterministic pass/fail for defined cases, clear failure classifications (decoder, schema-mapper, assembler, or tooling), and a small reproduction harness for local dev that can replay failing streams.

title: Schema evolution and rehydration for compressed structured logs with correlation expansion
preview:
Context:
We are ingesting increasingly dense structured logs from edge sidecars using a lossless compression + compact schema header. This increases throughput and reduces storage cost, but it makes ad-hoc queries, cross-tenant correlation, and historical reindexing difficult when schemas evolve. Recent customer debug sessions showed missing correlation fields when older compressors omitted optional correlation arrays.

Problem:
- Existing ingestion path stores a compressed payload blob with a compact schema id and field map. Query-time expansion requires the exact schema mapping; when schema evolves (field renames, optional arrays), queries return incomplete or missing 'corr_uid' and related join keys.
- Rehydration for observability workflows (alerts, slow-query traces) often needs field-normalized logs (expanded JSON) for Loki/ES indexing.
- Current migration attempts are manual and expensive (full reindex).

Proposal (high level):
1) Introduce a Rehydration Service that:
   - Streams compressed payloads from the raw S3/Kafka tier, decompresses using the schema registry, and emits expanded JSON to the canonical structured-log topic for downstream indexing.
   - Emits both expanded JSON and a lightweight differential patch (delta) for storage-efficient replays.
2) Extend the schema registry with immutable versioned mapping and a compatibility policy engine to compute canonical field names (e.g., canonical 'corr_uid').
3) Provide a query-time compatibility shim in Loki that can perform on-the-fly expansion for recent schemas (hot-path) and route older payloads to the Rehydration Service (cold-path).
4) Add a one-click rehydration job in the Console for customer-initiated backfills with safe throttles and snapshot checkpoints.

Design notes / specifics:
- Schema header v3 will include: schema_id, version, lightweight mapping table, and optional dedupe hints.
- Compression format: keep existing zstd framing but add a small versioned TLV after the frame with mapping_id. No change to payload bodies beyond the header.
- Rehydration Service will be a stateless consumer group with a small state store for mapping lookup and idempotent output via dedup token (topic key = tenant:schema_id:offset).
- Correlation expansion: for older schemas we will map deprecated correlation arrays into 'corr_uid' array using mapping rules in the registry. This ensures continuity for trace joins and alert rules.
- Rollout plan includes dark-writing expanded events for 2 weeks and keeping both compressed and expanded records for 30 days before retiring compressed-only queries.

Tradeoffs / risks:
- Cost: producing expanded JSON increases storage and network egress. Mitigations: delta-only retention, TTL policies, and rate-limited backfills.
- Latency: query-time shim may add latency for hybrid expansion. Mitigation: hot-path expansion and caching of recent mappings in Loki process.
- Data correctness: mapping mistakes can silently transform fields. Mitigation: strict compatibility checks and schema migration review workflow with sample previews in Console.

Acceptance criteria:
- Rehydration Service prototype decompresses and expands 95% of payloads in test corpus (50M logs) within 48h backfill window for a single consumer group with 10 instances.
- Loki query shim returns expected 'corr_uid' for the last 90 days on random sample queries (pass threshold >= 99% match vs. ground truth).
- Console exposes backfill job that completes with status updates and allows pause/resume.
- Migration docs and runbook created; security review completed.

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for mapping/rename logic and idempotent output.
- Integration test with compressed payload corpus from staging; automated diffs between expanded JSON and golden expanded set.
- Load test: rehydrate 100GB/day using kinesis-like producer to validate throughput and checkpointing.
- Observability: Grafana dashboard for rehydration lag, expansion error rate, per-tenant throughput, and storage delta trend.
text:
Context:
We are ingesting increasingly dense structured logs from edge sidecars using a lossless compression + compact schema header. This increases throughput and reduces storage cost, but it makes ad-hoc queries, cross-tenant correlation, and historical reindexing difficult when schemas evolve. Recent customer debug sessions showed missing correlation fields when older compressors omitted optional correlation arrays.

Problem:
- Existing ingestion path stores a compressed payload blob with a compact schema id and field map. Query-time expansion requires the exact schema mapping; when schema evolves (field renames, optional arrays), queries return incomplete or missing 'corr_uid' and related join keys.
- Rehydration for observability workflows (alerts, slow-query traces) often needs field-normalized logs (expanded JSON) for Loki/ES indexing.
- Current migration attempts are manual and expensive (full reindex).

Proposal (high level):
1) Introduce a Rehydration Service that:
   - Streams compressed payloads from the raw S3/Kafka tier, decompresses using the schema registry, and emits expanded JSON to the canonical structured-log topic for downstream indexing.
   - Emits both expanded JSON and a lightweight differential patch (delta) for storage-efficient replays.
2) Extend the schema registry with immutable versioned mapping and a compatibility policy engine to compute canonical field names (e.g., canonical 'corr_uid').
3) Provide a query-time compatibility shim in Loki that can perform on-the-fly expansion for recent schemas (hot-path) and route older payloads to the Rehydration Service (cold-path).
4) Add a one-click rehydration job in the Console for customer-initiated backfills with safe throttles and snapshot checkpoints.

Design notes / specifics:
- Schema header v3 will include: schema_id, version, lightweight mapping table, and optional dedupe hints.
- Compression format: keep existing zstd framing but add a small versioned TLV after the frame with mapping_id. No change to payload bodies beyond the header.
- Rehydration Service will be a stateless consumer group with a small state store for mapping lookup and idempotent output via dedup token (topic key = tenant:schema_id:offset).
- Correlation expansion: for older schemas we will map deprecated correlation arrays into 'corr_uid' array using mapping rules in the registry. This ensures continuity for trace joins and alert rules.
- Rollout plan includes dark-writing expanded events for 2 weeks and keeping both compressed and expanded records for 30 days before retiring compressed-only queries.

Tradeoffs / risks:
- Cost: producing expanded JSON increases storage and network egress. Mitigations: delta-only retention, TTL policies, and rate-limited backfills.
- Latency: query-time shim may add latency for hybrid expansion. Mitigation: hot-path expansion and caching of recent mappings in Loki process.
- Data correctness: mapping mistakes can silently transform fields. Mitigation: strict compatibility checks and schema migration review workflow with sample previews in Console.

Acceptance criteria:
- Rehydration Service prototype decompresses and expands 95% of payloads in test corpus (50M logs) within 48h backfill window for a single consumer group with 10 instances.
- Loki query shim returns expected 'corr_uid' for the last 90 days on random sample queries (pass threshold >= 99% match vs. ground truth).
- Console exposes backfill job that completes with status updates and allows pause/resume.
- Migration docs and runbook created; security review completed.

Testing plan:
- Unit tests for mapping/rename logic and idempotent output.
- Integration test with compressed payload corpus from staging; automated diffs between expanded JSON and golden expanded set.
- Load test: rehydrate 100GB/day using kinesis-like producer to validate throughput and checkpointing.
- Observability: Grafana dashboard for rehydration lag, expansion error rate, per-tenant throughput, and storage delta trend.

title: Conductorlet: admin proxy to translate runbooks into idempotent tasklets
preview:
Background:
Redwood on-call and support teams maintain canonical runbooks in a mixture of Markdown and YAML playbooks. Operators frequently need to run gated, idempotent steps from those runbooks in a repeatable way while preserving audit logs, RBAC checks, and telemetry for cost/latency impact.

Problem:
Current ad-hoc scripts and manual runbook execution cause variability: missing telemetry, inconsistent retries, and manual permission escalation. We need a small translator/proxy that enables runbook-as-task execution across admin tooling (CLI, Console, and API) while enforcing safety gates and emitting structured telemetry.

Proposal:
Build \"Conductorlet\" — a lightweight admin proxy service that: 1) ingests a constrained runbook YAML fragment and compiles it to typed, idempotent tasklets; 2) applies RBAC and approval policies; 3) routes tasks to the runlet execution runtime (existing runlet orchestration) with auto-retry and circuit breaker; 4) emits unified audit events and per-step telemetry; 5) surfaces a human-friendly preview and dry-run in the Console.

Design highlights and tradeoffs:
- Keep the translator stateless and deterministic so tasks are replayable; store provenance via an audit event that references the source runbook revision (git/drive).
- Use a conservative subset of runbook primitives (shell, api-call, kv-transform, wait, rollback) to avoid arbitrary code execution; heavy workflows continue to be authored as full runlets.
- Prefer compile-time validation (typed params, required approvals) over runtime checks where possible to reduce blast radius.
- Tradeoff: restricting primitives reduces flexibility but greatly simplifies safety review and attack surface.

Rollout plan:
1) Implement translator and local CLI integration for lightweight internal teams (canary group).
2) Add Console dry-run and per-task telemetry.
3) Gate public rollout behind security review and a 2-week canary with synthetic chaos tests.

Observability and SLOs:
- Each produced tasklet emits a structured audit event with fields: creator, source-runbook-id, tasklet-id, input-hash, execution-latency, attempts.
- SLOs: 99.9% tasklet acceptance within 300ms; 99.5% successful telemetry emission.

Open questions:
- Approval UX: inline modal vs. external ticket integration (Jira/Drive).
- How to version translator semantics so older runbook fragments remain replayable.

Next steps:
- Complete design doc and security checklist, then start implementation of the core translator library and API surface.

title: Route Adjudicator: incremental policy compiler and fallback-variant safety suite
preview:
Problem: The current routing policy codebase mixes validation, normalization, and runtime compilation in a single path which makes small changes risky and causes subtle regressions when introducing fallback model variants or new region affinity rules. We also lack an automated way to detect when a policy change will route traffic to an incompatible model variant (e.g., quantized variant without KV cache support), or when tier routing inadvertently starves lower-priority tiers during burst windows.

Goal: Implement a Route Adjudicator: an incremental policy compiler that separates parsing, typed-IR generation, and runtime contract checking. Deliver a fallback-variant safety suite that runs both static checks and lightweight runtime emulation to verify compatibility across: model capabilities, kv-cache expectations, quantization constraints, and regional deployment affinity. Output should be a machine-readable contract with compatibility flags and deterministic fallback order used by the serving runtime and orchestration layer.

title: Quantization profile lifecycle: FP8/INT8 calibration, canary rollout, and automated fallback policy
preview:
Goal: Add a robust lifecycle for quantization profiles (FP8 and INT8) that ties automated calibration and accuracy validation into a safe canary rollout with deterministic automatic fallback when quality checks fail. Context: Teams want to ship quantized model variants to reduce cost while avoiding regressions in user-facing metrics. We need a repeatable flow: calibration -> offline validation -> canary traffic -> live metric gating -> automated rollback/fallback to higher-precision variant if quality drops.

Scope:
- Define canonical quantization profile metadata and storage format for FP8/INT8 (including per-layer, per-op config and calibration seed).
- Implement a calibration job template (batchable) that produces candidate profiles and calibration stats (KL histograms, per-tensor max, activation ranges).
- Create an accuracy validation harness that runs candidate profiles against representative eval sets (classification, perplexity, NLL, task-specific metrics) and produces a quality report.
- Build canary rollout orchestration: route a small percent of production traffic to quantized variant, collect metrics, run statistical tests, promote or trigger fallback.
- Implement automatic fallback policy: deterministic criteria (metric thresholds + statistical significance + sample size) to switch routing to a compatible fallback model (higher-precision or unquantized) within SLOs.

Non-goals: automated retraining, per-customer custom datasets (will follow-up as separate initiative).

title: Chronal credit trading preemptor with adaptive KV compaction
preview: Introduce a new preemption policy (Chronal Credit Trading Preemptor) that assigns time-slice credits to inflight requests based on SLO slack and tenant weight, and allows soft-trading of credits between slices. Integrate this with an adaptive KV compaction/eviction component that considers expected reuse probability to reduce GPU memory pressure while preserving hot KV residency. The goal is to improve 95/99p latency SLOs under memory pressure and to provide a smoother fairness curve across tenants while limiting destructive KV churn.

title: Tiered commitment lanes and cutline simulator for API / Console / Runtime
preview:
Objective: Define a decision framework and a lightweight simulator that models staggered release lanes (Canary, Tier A reserved, Public) and enforces scope-lock / cutline criteria across API, Console, and Runtime services. Purpose is to reduce last-minute scope churn, standardize cross-team signals for go/no-go, and produce a canonical release notes payload for comms and docs teams.

Scope:
- Model three release lanes with configurable thresholds: latency P95 delta, error-rate increase, backend capacity headroom, and customer-impact flags.
- Capture stepwise cut criteria (feature freeze, doc freeze, last-call rolloffs) and map to owners.
- Provide a simple simulator (spreadsheet + runbook) to answer \"if metric X breaches at T-minus 3 days, which features cut and what comms runbook executes?\"
- Produce a release-notes skeleton and per-lane comms checklist that legal, support, and sales can consume.

Out of scope: automated gating in CI, deep observability instrumentation changes, or infrastructure-level traffic shaping (these will be linked as dependencies).

Approach:
1) Workshop with Eng/Design/Docs/Support to align on thresholds and acceptable mitigations.
2) Build a parametrized spreadsheet model + decision tree and validate it against two prior releases (runtime-1.18 and console-2025.11) to calibrate thresholds.
3) Draft a short runbook with ownership matrix and release-notes template.
4) Iterate with release managers and finalize canary windows and rollback criteria.

Success metrics: reduced last-minute scope cuts (target: 50% fewer ad hoc PRs removed after scope freeze), 30% faster production of release notes after GA, and consistent comms acceptance by sales/support orgs.

title: Token-surge priority-queue starvation & asymmetric circuit-breaker tripping postmortem
preview: Summary: On 2026-02-18 a sustained set of long-input requests originating from a single large customer (tokenization spike with many long sequences) caused priority queue starvation in the us-west-2 serving fleet. This produced asymmetric circuit-breaker tripping in secondary regions, uneven load shedding, and a 20-minute p99 SLO breach for hosted generation endpoints. This postmortem documents the timeline, root cause, mitigations applied during the incident, and follow-up action items to prevent recurrence.

title: Session-affinity precedence and safe rollout coordination for version pins across region/tier fallbacks
preview:
Problem: As we roll out model versions (canary/A-B) and allow session or user-level model pins, overlapping policy sources (admin pins, session pins, cohort assignment, geo/tier routing) create ambiguity about which model variant should serve a request when capacity or region constraints require fallback. This ticket defines deterministic precedence rules, session-affinity TTL semantics, and a safe degrade strategy that preserves rollout guarantees (cohort ratios) while enforcing region/tier constraints and minimizing customer-visible regressions.

Goal: Produce an implementable spec and integration tests for the routing resolver that enforce: (1) clear precedence among pin sources; (2) TTL-based session pin decay and re-assignment algorithm; (3) cohort preservation under geo/tier fallback (weight-preserving degrade); (4) automatic rollback thresholds and kill-switch integration; (5) observability hooks for per-cohort error/latency drift.

Acceptance criteria:
- Spec doc versioned at Confluence (link in comments).
- Resolver behavior implemented behind a feature flag.
- Integration tests that simulate region loss and verify cohort distribution approximates target weights after fallback.
- End-to-end canary with automatic rollback when cohort error-rate > 3x baseline for 10 minutes.
- Telemetry chart in Datadog showing request split by pin-source + cohort + fallback-reason.

Constraints and trade-offs: 
- Strict preservation of cohort ratios is expensive when cross-tier routing requires model variant substitution (e.g., quantized variant in lower tier). We'll prefer approximate weight-preservation where exact ratios are impossible.
- Deterministic behavior must be compatible with existing session sticky rules (session-id hashing) to avoid oscillation.
- Short TTLs increase exposure to routing churn; long TTLs delay rollout reach. We need a compromise TTL default and an adaptive decay for low-traffic sessions.

Out of scope: low-level kernel selection and quantization strategy (handled by runtime), developer-facing rollout UI updates (handled by Console team separately).

title: Design and implement dynamic scaling + throttling for embeddings and reranking paths
preview:
Problem: embedding and reranking traffic patterns have diverging latency and throughput requirements. Short reranking requests (many small vectors + similarity ops) can congest the embedding pool and trigger expensive cold-scaling events. Current autoscaler reacts to GPU utilization with high oscillation and no per-route prioritization, causing latency spikes for P0 customers and unexpected cost.

Goal: implement a deterministic, service-level aware scaling and throttling policy that: (1) separates capacity planning for embeddings vs reranking jobs, (2) adds adaptive queueing with backpressure and rate-limit tiers, (3) exposes config knobs for priority lanes and burst allowances, and (4) produces measurable improvements in p95/p99 latency and cost per embedding unit.

High-level approach:
- Introduce two logical request lanes inside the embedding service: "fast" (low-latency, low-batch reranking) and "bulk" (high-throughput embedding generation). Requests can be tagged by SDK/route or inferred by payload shape.
- Implement separate autoscaling policies per lane: fast lane scales aggressively to preserve single-request latency (target GPU util 40-60%), bulk lane prefers batching and higher util targets (70-85%).
- Add a lightweight in-service queue with adaptive admission control using token-bucket rate limits and dynamic queue depth tuned to observed KV cache reuse. For fast-lane hits, allow preemptive dequeueing; for bulk, allow larger batch windows.
- Introduce soft rate limits and graceful 429/Retry-After behavior for sustained bursts, with SDK guidance for exponential backoff and optional priority headers for enterprise customers.
- Capacity planning: add a forecasting job that ingests 14-day traffic histograms by route and emits instance and GPU-hour recommendations; integrate with Dedicated capacity console to surface recommended commits.
- Observability: new per-lane metrics (queue_depth, admitted_rate, throttled_rate, p50/p95/p99 latency, batch_size_distribution) and dashboard panels.

Implementation notes:
- Autoscaler: extend existing control loop to accept lane-specific targets and to use smoothed util windows (60s median + EWMA). Add hysteresis to avoid flapping.
- Queueing: implement lock-free ring buffer per worker with atomic counters; fallback to in-memory sharded queues if contention high.
- Kernel scheduling: ensure batcher groups fast-lane requests into micro-batches (<8 tokens) to avoid kernel path mismatches.
- SDK: add route metadata header "x-rw-embed-lane" and client-side guidance for retry/backoff.

Acceptance criteria: see acceptance_criteria field below for discrete checks.

- Queueing: implement lock-free ring buffer per worker with atomic counters; fallback to in-memory sharded queues if contention high.
- Kernel scheduling: ensure batcher groups fast-lane requests into micro-batches (<8 tokens) to avoid kernel path mismatches.
- SDK: add route metadata header "x-rw-embed-lane" and client-side guidance for retry/backoff.

Acceptance criteria: see acceptance_criteria field below for discrete checks.

title: Runtime path instability: capacity-latch causing p95 latency spikes and follow-ups
preview: During a weekend capacity surge on mixed-quant inference workloads we observed repeated p95/p99 latency spikes and sustained throughput degradation. The runtime entered a pathological state we are calling a 'capacity latch' where the scheduler consistently favored a slow fused-attention kernel variant for a subset of requests, causing microbatch pileup and eviction churn. This ticket contains the incident summary, reproduction notes, root-cause analysis, immediate mitigations applied, and prioritized action items to prevent recurrence.

title: Reduce 5xx waves by isolating long-lived streaming sessions and adjusting SDK retry semantics
preview:
Summary: Multiple enterprise customers reported intermittent 5xx bursts and elevated tail latency during peak hours. Investigation found a pattern where long-lived streaming sessions (websocket/streaming RPCs) from a small set of tenants were holding front-end connection slots and preventing efficient backpressure on the serving runtime. Combined with aggressive client retries and lack of per-tenant concurrent admission limits at the edge, this triggered connection pile-ups that led to proxy saturations, kernel backpressure in the runtime, and cascading 502/504 responses for unrelated tenants.

Impact: Several customers (hosted and dedicated) experienced degraded throughput and elevated error rates for ~2–8 minute windows. Customer escalations received from 3 enterprise accounts; one had partial feature outages in production.

Root cause (multifactor):
- Front door / edge allowed unbounded concurrent streaming connections per API key, so a misbehaving client could occupy many slots.
- The runtime's admission controller did not distinguish long-lived streaming sessions from short requests, leading to resource starvation for normal generation requests.
- SDK retry policy default (exponential without jitter + high max attempts) caused synchronized retries during transient slowdowns, amplifying the overload.
- KV-cache compaction was triggered under memory pressure; eviction churn increased latency for requests that depend on prefix cache warmness.

Decision and trade-offs:
- Implement per-tenant concurrent streaming cap at the edge (soft-limit with graceful queueing + hard-limit fast-fail). This reduces blast radius but risks rejecting some legitimate heavy-usage customers during bursts. We chose a conservative cap with telemetry-first rollout.
- Update SDK retry defaults to include randomized jitter and reduce max attempts; provide migration guidance to customers who require aggressive retry behavior. This is a client-side behavioral change but ship immediate server-side mitigations.
- Add lightweight admission priority for short generation requests vs. long streams in the runtime; short requests get a small reserved pool to protect latency-sensitive traffic. This requires modest changes to the scheduler but prevents head-of-line blocking.

Fix implemented:
- Edge change: admission proxy now enforces per-tenant streaming concurrency default = 4; soft queue up to 8 with exponential backoff headers. Tenancy config exposed to Dedicated customers. (PR: github.com/redwood/edge-proxy/pull/512)
- SDK change: bump JS/Python SDK default retry max_attempts -> 3, enable full jitter, and document idempotency token usage for safe retries. (PRs: github.com/redwood/sdk-js/pull/248, github.com/redwood/sdk-py/pull/142)
- Runtime change: scheduler reserve shards for short-path requests (20% of slot capacity), avoid using reserved slots for streams. (runtime-1.19 patch: github.com/redwood/runtime/pull/1189)
- Observability: new Grafana dashboard panels for per-tenant streaming concurrency, per-tenant retry amplification score, and KV-cache eviction rate. Alerts added for streaming concurrency > threshold and eviction spikes.

Verification and rollout: 
- Canary rollout on hosted region eu-west-1 for 48 hours; no customer-visible regressions observed, 70% reduction in 5xx spike frequency for canary tenants.
- Dedicated: opt-in config available; 3 pilot enterprise customers validated the behavior with safe defaults.
- SDKs published with deprecation notes for old retry behavior; communicated via changelog and targeted emails to customers who hit retry-related incidents.

Follow-ups / Postmortem actions:
- Post-incident audit to identify any customers who received fast-failures and offer guidance/credits.
- Engineering: create a small-run load test that simulates mixed streaming + short-request workloads to prevent regressions (owner: @lco').
- Product: include a recommended per-tenant streaming baseline in onboarding docs for enterprise customers.
- Security/Compliance: confirm telemetry changes do not leak customer PII and sign-off meta-logging.

Links to artifacts and PRs: 
- Incident timeline and notes: https://confluence.redwood.ai/pages/INC-2026-02-streaming-adm
- Edge proxy PR: https://github.com/redwood/edge-proxy/pull/512
- Runtime scheduler PR: https://github.com/redwood/runtime/pull/1189
- SDK JS PR: https://github.com/redwood/sdk-js/pull/248
- SDK PY PR: https://github.com/redwood/sdk-py/pull/142
- Grafana dashboard snapshot: https://grafana.redwood.ai/d/streaming-admission/streaming-admission-summary

preview: After the 2026-02-02 staged rollout of semantic-sim-2.1 to hosted customers, we observed a regression that escaped canary checks and resulted in elevated hallucination rates and increased token error responses. This ticket collates hypotheses from the on-call and post-incident review, captures agreed runbook and safety changes, and tracks follow-ups required to reduce blast radius for future staged deployments.

title: Ops Kiosk: microflow kiosk and contextual quick-fix executor
preview: Build a lightweight, searchable ops kiosk UI that exposes a curated set of small, idempotent microflows (pre-approved repair steps) for on-call and support engineers. The kiosk is intended to shorten mean-time-to-repair for common issues while preserving safety via RBAC, dry-run previews, and automatic audit logs.

title: Time-bound delegation: ephemeral role provisioning and automated access-evidence pipeline
preview: Problem: Customers require demonstrable, time-scoped access controls for auditors and internal security reviews across hosted and private deployments. Current RBAC and SCIM flows support static roles and periodic manual reviews; we lack a standardized mechanism for ephemeral (time-bound) delegations, automated SCIM lifecycle reconciliation for temporary grants, and an evidence export that ties sessions/approvals to attestation artifacts. Goal: Implement ephemeral role provisioning (JIT-style TTL grants), SCIM extensions for temporary memberships, SSO/SAML approval hooks for delegations, and an evidence pipeline that produces auditable exports linking request, approver, grant duration, and revocation events. This ticket covers design and core implementation for infra + control plane components required for rollout to Dedicated and Private customers.

title: Tokenization-aware latency buckets and SLO crediting telemetry
preview:
Problem: token-level latency SLIs currently assume a 1:1 token cost and uniform tokenization across models and regions. In practice, different tokenizers and model families result in widely varying token counts for the same semantic request. This skews per-token SLI calculation, SLO burn rates, and makes cross-model/regional SLO comparisons noisy.

Goal: implement instrumentation that buckets latency by effective tokenization granularity and emits SLO-crediting metrics that normalize for tokenizer differences. This will: 1) provide accurate per-request SLO credit accounting for multi-model routes; 2) enable regional SLO policies to account for tokenizer-induced variance; 3) improve attribution when circuit breakers/firewalls trigger load-shedding by isolating tokenization effects from serving-path latency.

Scope: telemetry only (no changes to circuit-breaking behavior). Produce dashboards and a synthetic prober that exercises diverse tokenization patterns. Deliverables: metric schema, ingestion pipeline rules, Grafana/Looker dashboard buildout, prober harness, and an SLO crediting mapping document for Runtime and Console teams.

title: Probe timing & KEDA cooldown interaction caused scale-down cascade and large cold-start spike
preview: Summary: On 2026-03-05 at ~03:42 UTC we observed a production incident affecting the inference request surface for our chat-routing service. Symptoms included a sudden spike in 5xx errors and 99th percentile latency for ~22 minutes for customers routed to region eu-west-1. The root cause was an interaction between misconfigured Kubernetes probes, an init-container that delayed readiness export of request metrics, and KEDA's scaler cooldown/stabilization settings which led to a rapid cascade of scale-downs to zero followed by slow scale-up (cold starts). This ticket documents the timeline, technical cause, immediate mitigations, and long-term action items to prevent recurrence.

title: Kernel Heuristics Latency-Fairness Consolidator
preview: Build a consolidated heuristic layer and lightweight autotuner that chooses kernels per-request to balance latency fairness across customers and sequence-length distributions. Unlike existing per-arch or per-seqlen rules, this consolidator combines short-term microbenchmark probes, online bandit signals, and historical seqlen/arch fingerprints to produce a latency- and cost-aware kernel choice with safe fallbacks for tail avoidance.

title: Request lifecycle anchor and KV stabilizer for async worker propagation
preview: Introduce a lightweight request lifecycle anchor header and a stable KV-mapping strategy to ensure spans and baggage survive boundary crossings through internal queueing systems, batchers, and long-running worker pools. This ticket implements SDK middleware and server-side request decorators to create a deterministic anchor ID, attach compact KV-stabilizer metadata, and rehydrate trace context in asynchronous consumers while preserving sampling fidelity.
